topic.what: Landmark Japanese prospective cohort study of 1526 patients followed for a mean of 7.8 years, demonstrating that gastric cancer developed exclusively in H. pylori-infected patients, with corpus-predominant atrophic gastritis as the highest-risk phenotype.
topic.how: Patients with various gastric conditions were enrolled and followed endoscopically; H. pylori status, gastritis pattern (antral vs corpus-predominant), atrophy, and intestinal metaplasia were assessed at baseline and correlated with cancer incidence.
topic.why_matters: Provided definitive prospective evidence that H. pylori infection is a necessary precondition for most gastric cancers and identified corpus atrophic gastritis and intestinal metaplasia as the key intermediate phenotypes driving cancer risk stratification.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Gastric cancer incidence in H. pylori-infected vs uninfected patients
characteristics.vs_baseline: Zero cancers in uninfected patients over 7.8-year follow-up
characteristics.evidence: Prospective cohort with serial endoscopic surveillance
characteristics.context: Japanese population, 1526 patients, mean follow-up 7.8 years

characteristics.what_it_measures: Relative risk of gastric cancer in patients with severe corpus atrophy
characteristics.value: Patients with corpus-predominant atrophic gastritis had significantly highest cancer risk
characteristics.vs_baseline: Compared to antral-predominant gastritis or no atrophy
characteristics.evidence: Subgroup analysis within the prospective cohort
characteristics.context: Stratified by gastritis topography and severity

characteristics.vs_baseline: Longest prospective H. pylori-cancer study at time of publication
characteristics.context: Sufficient duration to capture gastric cancer development

good_for: Gastric cancer risk stratification by gastritis phenotype
Justifying H. pylori eradication for cancer prevention
Evidence base for endoscopic surveillance guidelines

novelty: First large prospective study demonstrating zero gastric cancers in H. pylori-negative patients
Identified corpus-predominant atrophic gastritis as the highest-risk gastritis phenotype for cancer
Established that gastritis topography and severity are independent predictors of cancer risk
Provided the evidence base for risk-stratified surveillance approaches
open_problems: Whether eradication after atrophy/metaplasia is established can still prevent cancer (point of no return question)
Generalizability to low-prevalence Western populations uncertain
Molecular subtypes of gastric cancer not distinguished
Optimal surveillance interval for high-risk phenotypes not defined
